---
title: "Micro-frontends: ideas and trade-offs"
description: "Runtime composition, routing, and operational costs."
level: senior
order: 2
---

# Micro-frontends

```html
<!-- iframe integration: strong isolation, postMessage bridging -->
<iframe src="https://checkout.example.com/embed" title="Checkout"></iframe>
```

**Micro-frontends** try to apply microservice ideas to the browser: autonomous teams ship parts of the UI independently. An iframe is the blunt example of a hard boundary between teams.

## Integration

```js
// Module Federation (conceptual): host loads remote at runtime
// __webpack_require__.e("remote") → exposes "./Widget"
import("checkout/CheckoutApp");
```

You can compose with **iframes** (isolated but clunky), **Module Federation** (Webpack/Rspack), or **subdomain routing**, depending on team constraints and runtime. Each option changes how you share dependencies (React, design system) and versions.

## Trade-offs

```json
{
  "name": "shell",
  "dependencies": { "react": "^18.3.0" },
  "remotes": { "search": "search@https://cdn.example/search.js" }
}
```

You gain autonomy and speed per domain; you pay with **dependency duplication**, build complexity, and design consistency: two remotes can ship two Reacts if you do not coordinate.

```plaintext
single repo + package boundaries  →  “modular monolith”
```

<Callout title="Modular monolith">
Often a monorepo with strong boundaries and coordinated releases delivers most of the benefit with lower operational cost.
</Callout>
